text: The technique of "astroturfing" — manufacturing the appearance of grassroots political attention — reveals how attention itself has become a political commodity. PR firms since the 1990s have generated fake letter-writing campaigns, populated comment sections, and seeded social media with coordinated accounts designed to make fringe positions appear mainstream. The mechanism exploits a genuine cognitive heuristic: humans assess the credibility and urgency of a position partly by how many others seem to hold it. Political scientist Diana Mutz called this "impersonal influence" — citizens are moved not by arguments they evaluate directly but by their perception of what the broader population believes, a perception that can be systematically fabricated.
question: What cognitive mechanism does astroturfing exploit, according to the passage?

options.text: Citizens' susceptibility to emotionally charged language in political messaging
The tendency to evaluate positions based on how many others appear to hold them
The difficulty citizens have distinguishing online from offline social cues
The preference for visual information over text when forming political judgments
